title: Roast pork belly
subtitle: Slow-roasted pork belly with crispy crackling and fennel.
excerpt: Pork belly roasted slow and low after a high initial blast for crackling. Salted overnight or for at least two hours. Deeply seasoned with fennel seeds, garlic, and sea salt. The easiest and most rewarding Sunday roast.
sourceNotes: Pork belly has been eaten in Britain since at least the medieval period, when the whole pig was butchered and no cut went to waste. It fell out of fashion as a restaurant cut during the period of lean-meat preference in the mid-twentieth century but was revived by the gastropub movement of the 1990s and 2000s, which restored belly to the position of one of the most valued pork cuts for its fat content and flavour. The Italian porchetta tradition has influenced British roast belly preparations, particularly the use of fennel seeds.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Score and salt the skin the night before for better crackling.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for crackling blast; reduce to 160 °C

content.text: Crackling requires three things: dry skin, deep scoring, and a very high initial temperature. Salt draws moisture from the skin overnight. Scoring allows the heat to reach the fat beneath quickly. The high blast at the start of cooking sets the skin hard before the lower temperature takes over for the meat.

Score the skin in parallel lines 1 cm apart, cutting just through the skin into the fat below. Do not cut into the meat. A sharp knife is essential: blunt knives compress the skin rather than cutting through it.
A roasting pan and a sharp knife for scoring. A meat thermometer is optional but useful.

Score the skin of the pork belly in close parallel lines, cutting through the skin into the fat. Rub {{sea-salt-flakes}} of flaky salt vigorously into the scores and all over the skin. Place skin-side up on a plate, uncovered, in the fridge for at least 2 hours or overnight. The salt draws out moisture and the skin dries out, which is what produces good crackling.

Remove the pork from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Turn it over and rub the meat side with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{fennel-seeds}} of crushed fennel seeds, {{garlic}} of sliced garlic, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Turn back over skin-side up.

Heat the oven to 230 °C fan. Put the pork skin-side up in a roasting tin. Roast at 230 °C for 25–30 minutes until the skin is blistered and crackling. Reduce the oven to 160 °C fan and continue roasting for 1 hour 45 minutes until the meat is completely tender. Rest for 15 minutes before cutting.

items.symptom: Crackling is soft rather than crisp
items.cause: Skin was not dry enough before cooking, or the initial blast temperature was not high enough.
items.fix: Pat the skin completely dry with kitchen paper before the high blast. If crackling is still soft after cooking, remove it from the meat and place skin-side up under a hot grill for 3–5 minutes.

items.symptom: Crackling is burnt before the meat is cooked
items.cause: Oven temperature not reduced in time.
items.fix: Cover the crackling loosely with foil after the blast phase if it is colouring too fast. Remove the foil for the last 15 minutes.

items.cause: Bone-out belly is thinner and can dry out if overcooked.
items.fix: Check the thickest part of the meat: it should be tender and yielding. Bone-out belly typically needs less time than bone-in.
Use half the quantity next time. A single teaspoon is already subtle in the finished dish.

content.text: Roast pork belly arrived on British restaurant menus in the 1990s through chefs who had learned to value the cut for its fat content rather than apologise for it. The domestic version followed and pork belly is now one of the most popular Sunday roast choices, partly because it is inexpensive compared to loin or leg and partly because the technique of crackling-over-slow-roast is teachable and reliably produces impressive results. The fennel-seed seasoning is Italian in origin but has become part of the standard British treatment of the cut.
